---
title: "How Agentic Harnesses Work, Visually"
subtitle: "The machinery around the model: loops, tools, hooks, skills, MCP, memory, and agents that run for days"
author: "Pranjal Rawat"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: black
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    incremental: false
    slide-number: c/t
    controls: true
    progress: true
    width: 1200
    height: 720
    margin: 0.055
    center: true
    navigation-mode: linear
---

```{=html}
<style>
:root {
  --bg: #16171a; --fg: #e6e6e9; --muted: #8b8b92; --sub: #a3a3ab;
  --accent: #7cb0de; --feat: #6bbf8a; --amber: #d9a441; --rule: #2a2c31;
  --node: #1d2733; --edge: #5a6472; --ink: #0f1114; --pink: #e06b9c;
  --violet: #b19cf5;
}
.reveal { font-family: -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, "Segoe UI", Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif; }
.reveal, .reveal .slides section { background: var(--bg); color: var(--fg); }
.reveal h1, .reveal h2, .reveal h3 { color: var(--fg); font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: -0.5px; text-transform: none; }
.reveal h2 { font-size: 1.55em; border-bottom: 2px solid var(--rule); padding-bottom: 0.25em; margin-bottom: 0.5em; }
.reveal a { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal .slide-number { background: none; color: var(--muted); }
.reveal .footer, .reveal .slide-number { font-size: 12px; }
.reveal strong { color: var(--accent); }
.reveal em { color: var(--sub); font-style: italic; }
.reveal .subtle { color: var(--sub); font-size: 0.8em; }
.reveal .src { color: var(--muted); font-size: 0.55em; margin-top: 0.8em; }
.reveal .lead { font-size: 1.15em; line-height: 1.5; }
.reveal code { color: var(--feat); background: rgba(124,176,222,0.08); padding: 1px 6px; border-radius: 4px; }
.reveal section img { background: none; border: none; box-shadow: none; }
/* SVG shared visual language */
.fig { width: 100%; height: auto; max-height: 62vh; display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
.node { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; }
.node.feat { stroke: var(--feat); }
.node.hot { fill: #3a2f14; stroke: var(--amber); }
.node.ghost { fill: #191c20; stroke: var(--edge); }
.node.bad { stroke: var(--pink); }
.cap { fill: var(--fg); font: 16px -apple-system, sans-serif; text-anchor: middle; }
.cap.big { font-size: 21px; font-weight: 600; }
.zonelabel { fill: var(--muted); font: 600 14px -apple-system, sans-serif; letter-spacing: 1.5px; text-anchor: middle; }
.edge { stroke: var(--edge); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrow); }
.flow { stroke: var(--accent); stroke-width: 2.5; fill: none; marker-end: url(#arrowA); stroke-dasharray: 6 5; }
.zone { fill: none; stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 12; }
.card { fill: var(--node); stroke: var(--rule); stroke-width: 1.5; rx: 8; }
.card.hot { stroke: var(--amber); }
.card.ok { stroke: var(--feat); }
.card.bad { stroke: var(--pink); }
.cardtext { fill: var(--fg); font: 15px ui-monospace, monospace; }
.note { fill: var(--sub); font: 15px -apple-system, sans-serif; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.9); transition: all 0.3s ease; }
.reveal .fragment.grow-in.visible { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); }
/* draw-on edges */
.edge.draw, .flow.draw { stroke-dasharray: 1; stroke-dashoffset: 1; }
.reveal .fragment.visible .edge.draw,
.reveal .fragment.visible .flow.draw,
.reveal .edge.draw.fragment.visible,
.reveal .flow.draw.fragment.visible { animation: gv-draw 0.55s ease both; }
@keyframes gv-draw { from { stroke-dashoffset: 1; } to { stroke-dashoffset: 0; } }
.reveal .fragment.pop { opacity: 0; transform-box: fill-box; transform-origin: center; }
.reveal .fragment.pop.visible { animation: gv-pop 0.45s cubic-bezier(0.2,0.8,0.2,1) both; }
@keyframes gv-pop { 0% { opacity: 0; transform: scale(0.5); } 60% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1.06); } 100% { opacity: 1; transform: scale(1); } }
/* lecture callouts */
.reveal .def-box, .reveal .key-idea, .reveal .pitfall { background: var(--node); border-radius: 8px; padding: .5em .8em; margin: .4em 0; border-left: 4px solid var(--accent); font-size: .82em; line-height: 1.4; }
.reveal .key-idea { border-left-color: var(--feat); }
.reveal .pitfall  { border-left-color: var(--pink); }
.reveal .def-box::before  { content: "DEFINITION"; }
.reveal .key-idea::before { content: "KEY IDEA"; }
.reveal .pitfall::before  { content: "PITFALL"; }
.reveal .def-box::before,.reveal .key-idea::before,.reveal .pitfall::before { display:block; font-size:.62em; letter-spacing:1.5px; font-weight:700; color:var(--muted); margin-bottom:.25em; }
.reveal .taxonomy { display:grid; grid-template-columns:repeat(var(--cols,3),1fr); gap:.6em; margin-top:.5em; }
.reveal .taxonomy .cat { background:var(--node); border:1px solid var(--rule); border-top:3px solid var(--accent); border-radius:6px; padding:.5em .7em; font-size:.74em; }
.reveal .taxonomy .cat h4 { margin:0 0 .2em; color:var(--accent); font-size:1.05em; }
</style>

<svg width="0" height="0" style="position:absolute" aria-hidden="true">
  <defs>
    <marker id="arrow" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#5a6472"/>
    </marker>
    <marker id="arrowA" viewBox="0 0 10 10" refX="8.5" refY="5" markerWidth="7" markerHeight="7" orient="auto">
      <path d="M0,0 L10,5 L0,10 z" fill="#7cb0de"/>
    </marker>
  </defs>
</svg>
```

# One model, one shot {.center}

::: lead
A language model is astonishing for an hour and helpless over a week. It
forgets everything between sessions, grades its own homework, and stops
when the transcript looks finished, not when the work is.
:::

The harness is everything built around the model to fix that.

::: notes
This deck is about the machinery, not the model: the loop, the tools, the
hooks, the skills, MCP, the memory files, and the multi-session engines
that let an agent work for days. The sibling decks cover git and RAG; this
one covers the thing that runs them.
:::

---

## Ask for too much

One prompt, one model, twelve features. The model gets a single forward pass at a time, and a context window that does not grow with the job.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="40" y="150" width="300" height="100"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="62" y="185">"build todo.py with</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="62" y="210">all 12 features in</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="62" y="235">feature_list.json"</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 340 200 L 425 200"/>
  <text class="note fragment" data-fragment-index="2" x="383" y="180" text-anchor="middle">one shot</text>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="node" x="430" y="160" width="200" height="80" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="530" y="205">the model</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 630 200 L 715 200"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card bad" x="720" y="150" width="240" height="100"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="742" y="185" style="fill:var(--pink)">5 features built,</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="742" y="210" style="fill:var(--pink)">1 half-written,</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="742" y="235" style="fill:var(--pink)">context full</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="330">The task is bigger than any single context window</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the founding problem of the whole field: real work outlasts one
context. Everything in this deck follows from taking that seriously.
:::

---

## Shift workers with amnesia

Long work means many sessions. Anthropic's harness paper puts it plainly: each new session *"begins with no memory of what came before."*

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="zone" x="60" y="70" width="260" height="180"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="190" y="100">SESSION 1</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="190" cy="160" r="34"/>
    <text class="note" x="190" y="225" text-anchor="middle">starts blank</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="zone" x="370" y="70" width="260" height="180"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="100">SESSION 2</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="500" cy="160" r="34"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="225" text-anchor="middle">starts blank, again</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="zone" x="680" y="70" width="260" height="180"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="810" y="100">SESSION 3</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="810" cy="160" r="34"/>
    <text class="note" x="810" y="225" text-anchor="middle">and again</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="330">A project staffed by engineers on shifts,</text>
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="360">where nobody remembers the last shift</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: {.src}
Anthropic, Effective harnesses for long-running agents (2025)
:::

::: notes
The quote is verbatim from the keystone source. The shift-worker frame is
the single most useful mental model in the deck: every harness component
is something you would also build for human shift workers -- a logbook, a
task board, a test suite, a door that locks.
:::

---

## Meet tinyharness

Every figure ahead is a photograph of one real, executing system: a tiny Python harness wrapping a scripted model, building a real CLI app from a real 12-item feature list, with a real test suite as the judge.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="120" width="250" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="82" y="152">scripted model</text>
    <text class="note" x="82" y="182" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">a lookup table of turns,</text>
    <text class="note" x="82" y="200" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">so every run replays exactly</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="node" x="380" y="100" width="240" height="130" rx="12"/>
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="160">tinyharness</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="195" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">loop, tools, hooks, skills,</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="213" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">MCP, compaction, git -- all real</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card ok" x="690" y="120" width="250" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="712" y="152">todo.py + tests</text>
    <text class="note" x="712" y="182" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">12 features, unittest as</text>
    <text class="note" x="712" y="200" text-anchor="start" style="font-size:13px">the oracle, real git commits</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 310 165 L 375 165"/>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 620 165 L 685 165"/>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="320">The model's words are scripted. Everything else executes.</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="355" text-anchor="middle">Files really written, tests really run, hooks really block, commits really land. Nothing is a mockup.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
Same honesty contract as the sibling decks: the RAG deck runs a real toy
corpus, the git deck drives a real repository, this deck runs a real toy
harness. The one scripted part is the model's dialogue, which is what
makes every run reproducible and every figure assertable by code.
:::

---

<!--FB:the-problem|The Problem-->

---

# Part 1 · The Loop {.center}

::: lead
Start from the atom. A model call is a pure function of its context. Tools
give it hands. The loop gives it persistence.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
The harness is everything around the model that lets it work longer than one context window.
:::

::: {.src}
Grounding: docs/long-running-agents-research.md · autonomous-loop-patterns.md
:::

---

## Context window

Every model call sees exactly one thing: the column of text built up so far, system prompt, prior turns, tool results, nothing more.

::: {.def-box}
**Context window:** the column the model reads before each forward pass. It is the model's entire universe for that call, and it does not grow with the size of the job.
:::

---

<!--FB:one-call-one-context|One Call, One Context-->

---

## Tools give it hands

A model call alone cannot touch the disk. A tool is a named action, `read_file`, `write_file`, `run_tests`, that the model can ask for and the harness actually executes.

::: {.def-box}
**Tool:** a function the harness runs on the model's behalf. The model only proposes the call, the harness executes it, and the real result is what comes back into the context.
:::

---

<!--FB:tools-close-the-loop|Tools Close the Loop-->

---

## The agent loop

One session is a cycle repeated until the model stops or the budget runs out: read the state, act, check the real consequence, then write down what happened.

::: {.def-box}
**Agent loop:** orient (read what is true), act (call a tool), check (see the real result), record (persist the outcome), then repeat. Each lap feeds the next.
:::

---

<!--FB:the-agent-loop|The Agent Loop-->

---

## Anatomy of every harness

Strip away the branding and every long-running harness has the same six parts. The rest of this deck colors them in.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 480">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card hot" x="360" y="330" width="280" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="384" y="365">the disk</text>
    <text class="note" x="384" y="395" style="font-size:13px">files, git, the task board</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="node" x="60" y="90" width="220" height="80" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="170" y="125">driver loop</text>
    <text class="note" x="170" y="152" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">spawns sessions, holds the budget</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="node" x="390" y="90" width="220" height="80" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="125">fresh worker</text>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="152" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">new context every session</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="4">
    <rect class="node feat" x="720" y="90" width="220" height="80" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="830" y="125">verifier</text>
    <text class="note" x="830" y="152" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">fresh eyes, reruns the oracle</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="5">
    <rect class="node" x="130" y="220" width="220" height="70" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="240" y="250">stop control</text>
    <text class="note" x="240" y="275" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">budgets, strikes, Ralph checks</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="6">
    <rect class="node" x="650" y="220" width="220" height="70" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="760" y="250">human gate</text>
    <text class="note" x="760" y="275" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:13px">approvals, irreversible steps</text>
  </g>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="7" pathLength="1" d="M 250 170 L 450 330"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="7" pathLength="1" d="M 500 170 L 500 325"/>
  <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="7" pathLength="1" d="M 790 170 L 570 335"/>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="7">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="465">Every part reads and writes ONE shared thing: the disk</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: {.src}
Synthesis of ~35 public sources: Anthropic engineering, Cursor, Osmani, Huntley, 12-factor agents
:::

::: notes
The survey doc behind this slide read across every public long-running
harness it could find; these six parts appear in essentially all of them.
The disk being the hub, not any process, is the load-bearing fact.
:::

---

# Part 2 · Feeding the Context {.center}

::: lead
The context window is the model's entire universe, and it is small. Skills,
retrieval, and MCP are three disciplines for filling it with the right
things at the right time.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
Skills, RAG, and MCP are not separate features. They are three answers to the same question: what earns a place in a context window that never grows.
:::

---

## Skills load on demand

A skill is a packaged instruction set that only enters the context when the task actually matches it, in three tiers of cost.

::: {.def-box}
**Skill:** a one-line index every session pays for, a full body that loads once the task matches, and reference spokes that load only if the body actually points to them.
:::

---

<!--FB:skills-load-on-demand|Skills Load On Demand-->

---

<!--FB:rag-in-one-breath|RAG, in One Breath-->

---

## The full RAG story is its own deck

Chunking, embeddings as geometry, hybrid search, reranking, cite-or-refuse. All of it, slowly, over a real corpus.

[How RAG Works, Visually →](../../how-rag-works/website/rag.html)

---

## MCP, the universal port

MCP lets a separate program's tools show up in the model's tool belt, over a real JSON-RPC wire, indistinguishable from a built-in tool.

::: {.def-box}
**MCP (Model Context Protocol):** a JSON-RPC handshake, initialize, list tools, call a tool, that turns foreign code into something the model calls exactly like a native tool.
:::

---

<!--FB:mcp-the-universal-port|MCP, the Universal Port-->

---

# Part 3 · The Guardrails {.center}

::: lead
Why not just tell the model the rules? Because prose competes with
everything else in the column, and the model is graded on looking right.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
A rule the model can read is a rule it can lose to compaction or talk past. A rule wired as a hook fires at the loop boundary, every time, whether the model remembers it or not.
:::

---

## Why the model can't grade itself

One forward pass optimizes toward *looks approved*, not *is correct*. Four root causes, all structural, none fixable by asking nicely.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 440">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="node" x="80" y="60" width="200" height="80" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="180" y="95">one forward</text>
    <text class="cap" x="180" y="120">pass</text>
  </g>
  <path class="flow draw fragment" data-fragment-index="1" pathLength="1" d="M 280 100 L 680 100"/>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="card hot" x="690" y="65" width="250" height="70"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="712" y="95" style="fill:var(--amber)">"looks approved"</text>
    <text class="note" x="712" y="120" style="font-size:13px">not "is correct"</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card" x="60" y="200" width="200" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="78" y="232" style="font-size:13px">turn boundary is</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="78" y="252" style="font-size:13px">a one-way membrane</text>
    <text class="note" x="78" y="276" style="font-size:12px">no going back to check</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="card" x="290" y="200" width="200" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="308" y="232" style="font-size:13px">the preference</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="308" y="252" style="font-size:13px">prior</text>
    <text class="note" x="308" y="276" style="font-size:12px">trained to please</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="4">
    <rect class="card" x="520" y="200" width="200" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="538" y="232" style="font-size:13px">the verification</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="538" y="252" style="font-size:13px">gap</text>
    <text class="note" x="538" y="276" style="font-size:12px">claiming is cheaper than checking</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="5">
    <rect class="card" x="750" y="200" width="200" height="90"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="768" y="232" style="font-size:13px">per-turn</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="768" y="252" style="font-size:13px">statelessness</text>
    <text class="note" x="768" y="276" style="font-size:12px">no memory of its own promises</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="6">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="365">"Done" is not a report of a verified state.</text>
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="398">It is the most probable continuation of a transcript that looks finished.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: {.src}
docs/why-root-cause-2026-06-24.md, "Why the agent keeps making these mistakes"
:::

::: notes
The punchline is verbatim from the root-cause doc. This is the theory beat
of the deck; everything in Part 3 and Part 5 is a countermeasure to one of
these four causes.
:::

---

## Gates, not prose

A rule written in the context ages, drifts, and competes with everything else there. A rule wired as code fires exactly when it applies, every time.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 400">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="zone" x="60" y="60" width="400" height="270"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="260" y="90">RULE AS PROSE</text>
    <rect class="card" x="90" y="110" width="340" height="45"/>
    <text class="cardtext" x="110" y="138" style="font-size:13px">"please never touch .env"</text>
    <rect class="card ghost" x="90" y="165" width="340" height="30" style="opacity:0.7"/>
    <rect class="card ghost" x="90" y="203" width="340" height="30" style="opacity:0.5"/>
    <rect class="card ghost" x="90" y="241" width="340" height="30" style="opacity:0.3"/>
    <text class="note" x="260" y="310" text-anchor="middle">buried, compacted away, or just ignored</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="zone" x="540" y="60" width="400" height="270"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="740" y="90">RULE AS GATE</text>
    <rect class="node" x="580" y="130" width="140" height="60" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="650" y="165">tool call</text>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 720 160 L 790 160"/>
    <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
      <rect class="card bad" x="795" y="130" width="110" height="60"/>
      <text class="cardtext" x="815" y="165" style="fill:var(--pink)">DENIED</text>
    </g>
    <text class="note fragment" x="740" y="240" text-anchor="middle" data-fragment-index="3">fires at the exact moment the rule applies,</text>
    <text class="note fragment" x="740" y="265" text-anchor="middle" data-fragment-index="3">cannot be talked past, costs zero context</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
This is the pitch for hooks: deterministic code at the loop's boundary
beats instructions inside the stochastic part. The flipbook next shows a
real gate really blocking a real command.
:::

---

## Hooks own the boundary

A hook is code, not a suggestion in the prompt. It sits at a fixed point in the loop, before a tool runs, after one finishes, when the model tries to stop, and can inject text, deny the call outright, or bounce the session back to work.

::: {.def-box}
**Hook:** a callback wired to a loop event that returns a decision, allow, inject, deny, or continue, and the harness actually enforces it before the model's next move.
:::

---

<!--FB:hooks-own-the-boundary|Hooks Own the Boundary-->

---

# Part 4 · Memory Across Sessions {.center}

::: lead
The context dies with the session. Whatever should outlive it must move to
disk before the lights go out.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
The agent does not remember anything between sessions. The repo does. Whatever matters has to be written to disk before the context disappears.
:::

---

## Memory lives on disk

A fresh session starts with an empty context and no memory of the last one. What survives lives in files a new session reads back.

::: {.def-box}
**Memory file:** anything on disk a session writes before it ends, and a later session reads on the way in, git history, progress.md, feature_list.json, CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md.
:::

---

<!--FB:memory-lives-on-disk|Memory Lives on Disk-->

---

## Compaction

A session that runs long enough fills its own budget. Compaction is the harness stepping in to shrink the column before the model ever notices.

::: {.def-box}
**Compaction:** when the context passes its token budget, older turns are collapsed into one summary, keeping the earliest messages, that summary, and the most recent turns.
:::

---

<!--FB:compaction-what-survives|Compaction: What Survives-->

---

## Pitfall: what a summary drops

A constraint that rides into the context as an ordinary message can be the exact thing compaction summarizes away. The flipbook above is not hypothetical: the same rule, unpinned, survived right up until one squeeze later it was gone, and the next write really broke it.

::: {.pitfall}
An unpinned rule is one summary away from not existing. Pin what must survive compaction, and back it with a hook that checks the outcome rather than trusting the column still says so.
:::

---

## The machinery, at a glance

Five pieces do the work of getting the right thing into a small context window, and of keeping the model inside a boundary it cannot talk its way past.

::::: {.taxonomy style="--cols:5"}
::: cat
<h4>Tool</h4>
The harness executes, the model only asks. Real actions, real results back in the column.
:::
::: cat
<h4>Skill</h4>
Progressive disclosure. Index, body, spokes, loaded only as the task actually needs them.
:::
::: cat
<h4>MCP</h4>
A JSON-RPC port. Foreign code becomes a tool the model cannot tell from a built-in one.
:::
::: cat
<h4>Hook</h4>
A deterministic callback at a loop event. Injects, denies, or bounces, a gate not a suggestion.
:::
::: cat
<h4>Memory file</h4>
What outlives the session: git history, progress.md, feature_list.json, CLAUDE.md.
:::
:::::

---

# Part 5 · The Long Run {.center}

::: lead
Now assemble everything into a harness that ships a project across many
sessions: an initializer that builds the world, workers that do one feature
each, a verifier with fresh eyes, a driver that refuses early victory, and
a disk that survives any crash.
:::

::: {.key-idea}
Sessions are cattle, not pets. Kill one mid-task and the disk survives untouched. An initializer, one-feature sessions, a verifier, a Ralph loop, and kill-and-resume durability together turn many short, amnesiac sessions into one long-lived project.
:::

::: {.src}
Anthropic, Effective harnesses for long-running agents · Long-running Claude for scientific computing
:::

---

<!--FB:the-initializer|The Initializer-->

---

<!--FB:one-feature-per-session|One Feature per Session-->

---

<!--FB:four-failure-modes|Four Failure Modes-->

---

<!--FB:executor-vs-verifier|Executor vs Verifier-->

---

## Pitfall: grading your own homework

The executor above marked F10 green while its own test was failing. Not malice, just the structural bias from Part 3: a claim is cheaper to produce than a check.

::: {.pitfall}
An agent that self-certifies is not verified. Only a fresh agent with zero shared context, or a hard gate that reruns the real oracle, can confirm the work actually holds.
:::

---

<!--FB:the-ralph-loop|The Ralph Loop-->

---

<!--FB:kill-it-and-resume|Kill It, and Resume-->

---

## The long-run harness patterns

Five patterns, assembled, turn many short amnesiac sessions into one project that ships.

::::: {.taxonomy style="--cols:5"}
::: cat
<h4>Initializer</h4>
Session one builds the environment, not the app: feature list, run instructions, plan, log, tests, zero app code.
:::
::: cat
<h4>One-feature session</h4>
Get bearings, confirm the app is not already broken, ship exactly one feature, verify, commit, log.
:::
::: cat
<h4>Verifier</h4>
A second, fresh agent with zero shared context. Only artifacts cross the wall, it reruns the oracle instead of reading the claim.
:::
::: cat
<h4>Ralph loop</h4>
A stop hook that re-reads the feature list from disk on every stop attempt and bounces the session until the oracle says done.
:::
::: cat
<h4>Kill-and-resume</h4>
Nothing precious lives in the process. SIGKILL a session and the disk survives, a fresh session resumes only what was interrupted.
:::
:::::

---

# The Whole Machine {.center}

---

## Many brains, many hands

At fleet scale the same shapes recur. Flat self-coordinating agents deadlock; planner, worker, and judge scale.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 420">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="zone" x="50" y="60" width="270" height="280"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="185" y="90">FLAT</text>
    <circle class="node" cx="120" cy="160" r="24"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="250" cy="160" r="24"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="185" cy="250" r="24"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 144 160 L 226 160"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 135 180 L 172 230"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 235 180 L 198 230"/>
    <text class="note" x="185" y="315" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--pink)">risk-averse, deadlocks on locks</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="zone" x="365" y="60" width="270" height="280"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="500" y="90">PLANNER / WORKER / JUDGE</text>
    <rect class="node" x="440" y="110" width="120" height="46" rx="8"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="139" style="font-size:14px">planner</text>
    <rect class="node" x="390" y="195" width="100" height="42" rx="8"/>
    <rect class="node" x="510" y="195" width="100" height="42" rx="8"/>
    <text class="cap" x="440" y="221" style="font-size:13px">worker</text>
    <text class="cap" x="560" y="221" style="font-size:13px">worker</text>
    <rect class="node feat" x="440" y="270" width="120" height="46" rx="8"/>
    <text class="cap" x="500" y="299" style="font-size:14px">judge</text>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 470 156 L 445 195"/>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 530 156 L 555 195"/>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 445 237 L 480 270"/>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="2" pathLength="1" d="M 555 237 L 520 270"/>
    <text class="note" x="500" y="330" text-anchor="middle" style="fill:var(--feat)">continue or stop is the judge's call</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="zone" x="680" y="60" width="270" height="280"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="815" y="90">BRAIN / HANDS</text>
    <rect class="node" x="745" y="120" width="140" height="55" rx="8"/>
    <text class="cap" x="815" y="152" style="font-size:14px">session state</text>
    <rect class="node" x="745" y="195" width="140" height="55" rx="8"/>
    <text class="cap" x="815" y="227" style="font-size:14px">harness loop</text>
    <rect class="node" x="745" y="270" width="140" height="55" rx="8"/>
    <text class="cap" x="815" y="302" style="font-size:14px">sandbox</text>
    <text class="note" x="815" y="358" text-anchor="middle">three interfaces, swappable hands</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="4">
    <text class="note" x="500" y="395" text-anchor="middle">One dissent worth keeping: parallel agents without shared context fragment decisions (Cognition).</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: {.src}
Cursor, Scaling long-running autonomous coding · Anthropic, Scaling managed agents
:::

::: notes
Cursor's flat experiment produced agents that deadlocked on file locks and
grew risk-averse; the planner/worker/judge pipeline shipped a browser-scale
codebase. Managed Agents decouples the brain from the hands so sessions
are cattle, not pets.
:::

---

## The whole machine

Every element the deck introduced, on one map.

```{=html}
<svg class="fig" viewBox="0 0 1000 500">
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="1">
    <rect class="zone" x="330" y="40" width="360" height="230"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="510" y="66">ONE SESSION</text>
    <rect class="card" x="355" y="85" width="130" height="160"/>
    <text class="note" x="420" y="110" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px">context</text>
    <rect x="365" y="120" width="110" height="14" style="fill:#8b8b92; opacity:0.6; rx:3"/>
    <rect x="365" y="139" width="110" height="14" style="fill:#b19cf5; opacity:0.6; rx:3"/>
    <rect x="365" y="158" width="110" height="14" style="fill:#7cb0de; opacity:0.6; rx:3"/>
    <rect x="365" y="177" width="110" height="14" style="fill:#e8834a; opacity:0.6; rx:3"/>
    <rect x="365" y="196" width="110" height="14" style="fill:#6bbf8a; opacity:0.6; rx:3"/>
    <circle class="node" cx="580" cy="130" r="30"/>
    <text class="cap" x="580" y="135" style="font-size:13px">model</text>
    <rect class="node" x="545" y="190" width="70" height="40" rx="8"/>
    <text class="cap" x="580" y="215" style="font-size:12px">tools</text>
    <path class="edge" d="M 485 140 L 550 130"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 580 160 L 580 190"/>
    <path class="edge" d="M 545 215 L 490 210"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="2">
    <rect class="card hot" x="640" y="95" width="40" height="120"/>
    <text class="note" x="660" y="240" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:12px; fill:var(--amber)">hooks</text>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="3">
    <rect class="node" x="80" y="90" width="170" height="60" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="165" y="118" style="font-size:14px">skills</text>
    <text class="note" x="165" y="140" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:11px">index → body → spokes</text>
    <rect class="node" x="80" y="175" width="170" height="60" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="165" y="203" style="font-size:14px">MCP servers</text>
    <text class="note" x="165" y="225" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:11px">JSON-RPC tool ports</text>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 250 120 L 352 130"/>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="3" pathLength="1" d="M 250 205 L 540 212"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="4">
    <rect class="card hot" x="290" y="320" width="440" height="110"/>
    <text class="zonelabel" x="510" y="348">THE DISK</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="315" y="378" style="font-size:12px">feature_list.json · progress.md · AGENTS.md</text>
    <text class="cardtext" x="315" y="402" style="font-size:12px">todo.py · tests/ · .git (commit heartbeat)</text>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="4" pathLength="1" d="M 510 270 L 510 315"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="5">
    <rect class="node feat" x="770" y="320" width="160" height="70" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="850" y="350" style="font-size:14px">verifier</text>
    <text class="note" x="850" y="372" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:11px">fresh eyes, rerun the oracle</text>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="5" pathLength="1" d="M 770 355 L 735 370"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment pop" data-fragment-index="6">
    <rect class="node" x="80" y="320" width="160" height="70" rx="10"/>
    <text class="cap" x="160" y="350" style="font-size:14px">driver + Ralph</text>
    <text class="note" x="160" y="372" text-anchor="middle" style="font-size:11px">budgets, strikes, "really done?"</text>
    <path class="edge draw fragment" data-fragment-index="6" pathLength="1" d="M 240 355 L 285 370"/>
  </g>
  <g class="fragment" data-fragment-index="7">
    <text class="cap big" x="500" y="475">Sessions come and go. The disk is the project.</text>
  </g>
</svg>
```

::: notes
The assembled map mirrors the anatomy slide from Part 1, now with every
box filled in by a flipbook the viewer has watched actually run.
:::

---

## The harness carries the intelligence

A stronger model helps. A stronger harness helps more, because every component encodes something no model can do alone: remember, verify, refuse, survive.

::: lead
*"The agent doesn't get smarter across iterations. The codebase gets
smarter, and the next agent reads it."*
:::

Existence proofs: a C compiler built over ~2,000 fresh sessions, each reading the repo cold. A scientific solver run for weeks on checkpoints and an oracle. Neither needed a bigger model. Both needed a better harness.

::: {.src}
Quote: Geoffrey Huntley, the Ralph technique · docs/long-running-agents-research.md · Anthropic, Long-running Claude for scientific computing
:::

---

## References

**Anthropic engineering**
[Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents) · [Long-running Claude for scientific computing](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering) · [Scaling managed agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering)

**Practitioners**
[Cursor: scaling long-running autonomous coding](https://cursor.com/blog) · [Addy Osmani: self-improving coding agents](https://addyosmani.com) · [Geoffrey Huntley: the Ralph technique](https://ghuntley.com/ralph/) · [12-factor agents](https://github.com/humanlayer/12-factor-agents)

**Siblings**
[How Git Works, Visually](../../how-git-works/website/git.html) · [How RAG Works, Visually](../../how-rag-works/website/rag.html) · [How Inference & Serving Work, Visually](../../how-inference-and-serving-works/website/inference.html) · [How Evals & Observability Work, Visually](../../how-evals-and-observability-work/website/evals.html) · [How LangChain & LangGraph Work, Visually](../../how-langchain-and-langgraph-work/website/langchain.html)

::: {.src}
Every figure in this deck: a real run of tinyharness, regenerable with build/regen_all.py
:::
